heading: LVIII SAVERIO CAMPANINI
card_summary: This chunk is Campanini's editorial commentary (not Zorzi's text directly) analyzing Zorzi's allegorical treatment of the pagan god Pan in De Harmonia Mundi. Campanini traces Zorzi's composite sources — Homer's Hymns to Pan, Orphic hymns, Servius, Macrobius, Boccaccio's Genealogiae deorum gentilium, Pico della Mirandola's Conclusiones, and Hermes Trismegistus's Liber XXIV philosophorum — to show how Zorzi refuses Boccaccio's dichotomy (Pan as nature or sun) and instead identifies Pan simultaneously with nature and with God as "sole soprasensibile." The commentary then pivots to Zorzi's treatment of the ineffable divine Name (Tetragramma), examining how his reduction of YHWH to three non-repeated letters serves a Trinitarian apologetic inherited from the converso controversialist Pietro Alfonsi and later taken up by Pietro Galatino and Reuchlin in Christian Kabbalistic contexts.
essay:
# Campanini's Commentary on Zorzi's Allegory of Pan and the Divine Name

## Overview

Chunk 12 (pp. 58–62) consists entirely of Saverio Campanini's editorial apparatus — a sustained philological and intellectual-historical commentary on two related sections of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*: (1) the allegorical figure of Pan as a cosmic image, and (2) the Kabbalistic speculation on the divine Tetragramma. Campanini's method throughout is source-critical: he reconstructs the genealogy of each allusion, measures Zorzi's selectivity, and situates him within the broader Renaissance tradition of mythographic synthesis.

---

## Pan as Cosmic Allegory: The Problem of Sources

Campanini opens by naming the literary tradition behind Zorzi's invocation of Pan. When Zorzi cites "i poeti," the reference is complex: Virgil lies in the background, but so do the *Inni a Pan* attributed to Homer and to Orpheus. The popular etymology linking Pan to the Greek "tutto" (the all) is attested in Hellenistic-Roman texts and travels through medieval and Renaissance allegory via two pivotal texts: **Servius's Commentary on the Eclogues** (which becomes Zorzi's proximate source) and **Macrobius's Saturnalia**.

Campanini traces the further allegorical tradition through Isidore of Seville, Rabanus Maurus, Alberico's *Allegoriae poeticae*, and — crucially — **Boccaccio's *Genealogiae deorum gentilium***. Boccaccio presented two interpretive possibilities: Pan as nature, or Pan as the sun. Zorzi's characteristic move is to refuse this dichotomy:

> Zorzi rifiuta la dicotomia e, secondo il suo stile, identifica la figura di Pan con la natura ma, allo stesso tempo, con Dio, che può essere metaforicamente chiamato 'sole soprasensibile', salvando così l'interpretatio presente in Macrobio.

<cite>p. 58</cite>

This synthesis — Pan = nature = God as supersensible sun — is typical of Zorzi's "accumulative strategy," which Campanini treats as a defining feature of the *De Harmonia Mundi*'s compositional method.

---

## Pico, Signorelli, and the Florentine Atmosphere

Campanini situates Zorzi's Pan within the Florentine Neoplatonic milieu. He cites **Pico della Mirandola's Conclusio 28** (*Conclusiones secundum propriam opinionem de modo intelligendi hymnos Orphei secundum Magiam*):

> Frustra adit naturam et Protheum, qui Pana non attraxerit.

<cite>p. 59 (Pico cited by Campanini)</cite>

Though Zorzi almost certainly never saw **Luca Signorelli's** *Pan deus Arcadiae* (painted for Lorenzo de' Medici, destroyed at Berlin in 1945), Campanini invokes the painting as evidence of the same meditative atmosphere that shaped Zorzi's thinking. The image of Pan circulates from Poliziano through Pierio Valeriano's *Hieroglyphica* to Francis Bacon, and even to the chorus of nymphs in Goethe's *Faust*.

Campanini contrasts Zorzi's approach with that of **Cristoforo Landino** (in his *Commento alle Ecloghe di Virgilio*), who — following Servius and aware of Plutarch's account of the death of Pan (known via Eusebius of Caesarea) — explicitly noted that the passage about Pan's death did not fit his context (*neque huic loco quadrat*). Zorzi, by contrast, uses the death of Pan elsewhere in the *De Harmonia Mundi* to mark the cessation of pagan oracles at the time of the Passion of Christ, and in the later *Problemata* as evidence that demons are born and die.

---

## Zorzi's Compositional Method: Accumulation and Selective Silence

Campanini offers an extended methodological observation about how Zorzi constructs arguments:

> Il meccanismo compositivo di Zorzi diventa allora più chiaro: un gran numero di fonti viene convocato a suggellare la grande intuizione di base, ma occorre leggere in controluce la superficie argomentativa per scoprire che i passi tralasciati, ridimensionati o passati sotto silenzio sono almeno altrettanto numerosi.

<cite>p. 61</cite>

This is illustrated by Zorzi's use of **Eusebius of Caesarea's *Praeparatio Evangelica***. Eusebius had cited **Porphyry's *Peri agalmaton*** (On Cult Statues), making Pan a symbol of *mystica theologia* — but had then refuted Porphyry's interpretation by identifying Pan with a demon, thus paving the way for the conventional iconography of the devil with goat's legs. Zorzi draws on Eusebius selectively: he takes the Porphyrian allegorization without mentioning Eusebius's demonic identification.

Similarly, from Pan's erotic mythology Zorzi retains only the already-sublimated figure of **Syrinx** (as flute, not as Ovidian seduction narrative) and the figure of **Echo**, interpreted via Macrobius as God's delight in hearing the reverberation of his own creative word — a fusion of pagan mythology with the biblical theme of the divine creative Word.

The allegorical Pan is then combined with **Proclean philosophical theorems** on first causes and — attributed to **Hermes Trismegistus** — with the definition from the **Liber XXIV philosophorum**: God as an infinite sphere whose center is everywhere and whose circumference is nowhere. This Hermetic image closes the first argumentative circle, saluted with homage to **Marsilio Ficino** (his translation of the *Liber de Platonis definitionibus* of Speusippus) and a reverse-chronological triad of **Plato, Boethius, and Augustine** — a chain, Campanini notes, that marks the passage from ancient to Christian philosophy in deliberately inverted order. The influence of Zorzi's Pan meditation is traced forward to **Giulio Camillo Delminio's *Idea del theatro***.

---

## The Tetragramma and Christian Kabbalistic Controversy

The second argumentative arc shifts to the divine Name. Campanini frames Zorzi's speculation on the Tetragramma (YHWH) as a "fulminante sintesi" of well-known Christian Hebraism, largely dependent on Ficino rather than original Kabbalistic engagement. The key move is Zorzi's reduction of the four letters of YHWH to three non-repeated letters (since *he* is repeated), which Campanini identifies as a longstanding controversialist gambit aimed at deducing the mystery of the Trinity from the structure of the Hebrew divine Name.

Campanini notes that Zorzi elsewhere uses all four letters when the cosmological context demands it (discussing the four elements), citing a passage directly:

> "Dunque, queste quattro fondamenta di tutte le cose provengono da Dio, il cui nome ha quattro lettere, ciascuna delle quale corrisponde a un numero circolare e perciò quelle lettere comprendono tutti i numeri che sono detti circolari. Qui si annuncia a noi un grande mistero: ogni ciclo di generazione, corruzione e trasformazione delle cose deve alla fine far ritorno a ciò da cui prese le mosse in principio."

<cite>p. 62 (Zorzi quoted within Campanini's apparatus)</cite>

The genealogy of the three-letter Trinitarian argument is traced to **Pietro Alfonsi** (*quondam Moshe Sefardi*, converted Jew, d. ca. 1110–1130), author of the *Dialogus Petri cognomento Alphonsi, ex Judaeo Christiani, et Moysi Judaei*, identified as the founding text of a rich polemical literature. From Alfonsi the tradition runs through the medieval anti-Jewish controversialists to the Christian Kabbalists most visible to Zorzi: **Pietro Galatino** and **Johann Reuchlin**.

---

## Significance for Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk illuminates two structural features of the *De Harmonia Mundi*. First, Zorzi's "accumulative and selective" method: sources are layered to reinforce a grand intuition, while inconvenient passages (Eusebius's demonization of Pan, Ovid's erotic narratives) are quietly suppressed. Second, the concentric argumentative structure: the Hermetic sphere-image of the divine leads organically into Kabbalistic Name-speculation, the two forming interlocking circles that both arrive at the same ineffable center. Campanini's commentary throughout emphasizes that Zorzi's apparent erudition masks a deeply tendentious selectivity in service of his Christian-Kabbalistic synthesis.
